title: Project: Build the Community Website History
summary: Create a readable sequence of cohesive commits for a small community website instead of saving unrelated changes in one snapshot.

sketchBlocks.title: Building a Cohesive Commit History
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: In this project, you'll work as a junior web maintainer for a community center website. The goal is to create a readable commit history that clearly documents changes to the website's home page and events page. This will help other developers understand the updates without needing to open every file. You'll learn to use Git commands to inspect changes, stage them selectively, and commit them with meaningful messages. By the end of this project, you'll have a clean, organized commit history that reflects the website's development accurately.

sketchBlocks.title: Using Git Diff to Inspect Changes
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: Imagine you've made some changes to the community website's navigation bar in `index.html`. Before committing, it's crucial to inspect these changes to ensure they are correct and complete. Use `git status` to see which files have been modified. Then, use `git diff` to view the specific changes in `index.html`. This command shows line-by-line differences between the working directory and the last commit, helping you verify the changes before staging them.

sketchBlocks.title: Selective Staging with Git Add
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: After inspecting the changes in `index.html`, you decide to stage only the navigation updates. Use `git add index.html` to stage these changes. It's important to stage only related changes to keep commits focused. You can verify what's staged by using `git diff --staged`, which shows the differences between the staged files and the last commit. This ensures that only the intended changes are included in the next commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Creating Meaningful Commits
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: With the navigation changes staged, it's time to commit them. Use `git commit -m "Add primary navigation"` to create a commit with a clear, descriptive message. This message should convey the purpose of the changes, making it easier for others to understand the commit's intent. A well-written commit message is crucial for maintaining a readable project history.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspecting Commit History with Git Log
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: Worked example: After making several commits, you can review the project's history using `git log --oneline`. This command provides a concise view of the commit history, showing commit hashes and messages. To inspect a specific commit in detail, use `git show <commit-hash>`. This reveals the changes made in that commit, helping you understand the project's evolution over time.

quizDraft.title: Inspect the First Website Change
quizDraft.prompt: Use `git status` and `git diff` to identify the changes made to `index.html` without staging them. Type the commands and press Enter to see the output.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git status` to see modified files and `git diff` to view changes.

help.concept: Inspecting changes before staging helps ensure accuracy and focus.
help.hint_1: Check which files are modified with `git status`.
help.hint_2: Use `git diff` to see the specific changes in `index.html`.

quizDraft.title: Stage Only the Navigation Update
quizDraft.prompt: Stage the changes in `index.html` for the navigation update. Use `git add` and verify with `git diff --staged`. Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Stage the file with `git add` and verify with `git diff --staged`.

help.concept: Staging specific changes helps keep commits focused and organized.
help.hint_1: Use `git add index.html` to stage the file.

quizDraft.title: Commit the Navigation Update
quizDraft.prompt: Commit the staged navigation changes with a meaningful message. Use `git commit` and verify that `events.html` still has an unstaged change. Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git commit -m` to create a commit with a message.

help.concept: Committing with clear messages helps maintain a readable history.
help.hint_1: Create a commit with `git commit -m "Add primary navigation"`.

quizDraft.title: Prepare the Events Update
quizDraft.prompt: Finish the events update in `events.html`, inspect its unstaged diff, and stage only `events.html`. Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Edit `events.html`, then use `git diff` and `git add`.

help.concept: Staging only related changes keeps commits focused.
help.hint_2: Use `git diff` to inspect changes and `git add events.html` to stage.

quizDraft.title: Commit the Events Update
quizDraft.prompt: Commit the staged events changes with a meaningful message. Use `git commit` and ensure the navigation commit is preserved. Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git commit -m` to create a commit with a message.

help.concept: Committing with clear messages helps maintain a readable history.
help.hint_1: Create a commit with `git commit -m "Publish weekend events"`.
help.hint_2: Ensure the previous navigation commit is preserved.

quizDraft.title: Read and Verify the Finished History
quizDraft.prompt: Use `git log --oneline` and `git show` to inspect the two new commits. Ensure the working tree is clean. Type the commands and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git log --oneline` to view history and `git show` for details.

help.concept: Reading commit history helps understand project evolution.
help.hint_1: Use `git log --oneline` to see commit summaries.
help.hint_2: Use `git show <commit-hash>` to inspect specific commits.
